example: Statement: 'Neenu hoguttiya.' (You're going.) Question: 'Neenu hoguttiya?' (Are you going?) — Same words, different melody.

action: Learn 10 place/building words: Angadi (shop), Aaspathre (hospital), Shale (school), Devaalaya (temple), Masjid (mosque), Church, Bank, Post office, Police station, Petrol bunk.
description: Learn 10 place and building words. Combined with direction words, these let you ask for and give directions to anywhere.
context: Place words + 'yelli?' (where?) = navigation. 'Aaspathre yelli?' (Where's the hospital?) could be critical someday.
constraint_note: Make 5 'where is' questions: 'Bank yelli?', 'Shale yelli?'. Then give directions using words you know.
example: ಅಂಗಡಿ (Angadi)=shop, ಆಸ್ಪತ್ರೆ (Aaspathre)=hospital, ಶಾಲೆ (Shale)=school. 'Hattira-dalli angadi ide?' = Is there a shop nearby?

items.body: Pronounced AN-ga-di. Shop/store. 'Angadi-ge hogbeku' = need to go to the shop. 'Batte angadi' = cloth shop. 'Angadi beedhi' = market street. In Bangalore, 'Commercial Street' is still called 'angadi beedhi' by locals. A fundamental word for daily errands.

Pronounced AAS-pat-re. Hospital. 'Aaspathre-ge hogbeku' = need to go to hospital. Borrowed from English 'hospital' but fully Kannadized. 'Aaspathre-yalli' = in the hospital. Knowing this word is important for emergencies — 'hattira aaspathre yelli?' = where is the nearest hospital?

Pronounced SHAA-le. School. 'Shaale-ge hogu' = go to school. 'Shaale magu' = school child. 'Shaale chutti' = school holiday. Education is highly valued in Karnataka — 'shaale' is one of the first words children learn to write. 'Shaale master' = school teacher.

action: Learn to express continuous/progressive tense: 'verb + uttiddene' (I am doing). 'Naanu ooduttiddene' (I am reading), 'Avanu baruttiddane' (He is coming).
description: Learn the continuous tense — 'I am doing' vs 'I do.' This describes actions happening right now.
context: Continuous tense is what you need for real-time description: 'Naanu kelsa maaduttiddene' (I'm working right now) — useful for phone calls and messages.
constraint_note: Conjugate 3 verbs in continuous tense for all pronouns. Make 3 sentences about what you're doing right now.
example: ನಾನು ಓದುತ್ತಿದ್ದೇನೆ (Naanu oduttiddene)=I am reading. ನಾನು ಕೆಲಸ ಮಾಡುತ್ತಿದ್ದೇನೆ (Naanu kelsa maaduttiddene)=I am working.

action: Learn phone conversation phrases: 'Hello, yaaru maatadtiddiru?' (Who's speaking?), 'Swalpa kaali' (Hold on), '___ iddara?' (Is ___ there?), 'Naanu ___ maatadtiddene' (This is ___), 'Nantara phone maadi' (Call later), 'Message maadi' (Send a message).
description: Learn 6 phone conversation phrases. Phone calls in Kannada are common — delivery people, building staff, local services.
context: Phone Kannada is harder than face-to-face because you can't use gestures. Having set phrases ready makes phone calls manageable.
constraint_note: Practice a phone call: answer, identify yourself, ask for someone, say you'll call back. Say aloud.
example: Hello, yaaru maatadtiddiru? Naanu Abhishek maatadtiddene. ___ iddara? Swalpa kaali. Nantara phone maadi.

action: Write the complete Kannada consonant grid from memory: all 5 rows (ka, cha, Ta, ta, pa groups) + semi-vowels and sibilants.
description: Test yourself: write the entire Kannada consonant set from memory. This is the ultimate script recall test.
context: If you can write the full consonant grid from memory, you truly know the Kannada alphabet. This is a milestone.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write all 5 rows of 5, then the 9 additional consonants. Check after. Celebrate what you got right.
example: Row 1: ಕ ಖ ಗ ಘ ಙ. Row 2: ಚ ಛ ಜ ಝ ಞ. Row 3: ಟ ಠ ಡ ಢ ಣ. Row 4: ತ ಥ ದ ಧ ನ. Row 5: ಪ ಫ ಬ ಭ ಮ. Plus: ಯ ರ ಲ ವ ಶ ಷ ಸ ಹ ಳ.

action: Learn about 'Kaage' culture — the Kannada tradition of sharing food. Learn the phrase 'Swalpa tinniri' (Please eat a little) and when it's used.
description: Understand the Kannada food-sharing culture. Offering food is a deep social norm — refusing too quickly can seem rude.
context: In Karnataka, offering food is an expression of care. 'Swalpa kaapi kudiyiri' (Please have some coffee) is not just politeness — it's warmth. Accept graciously.
constraint_note: Learn: 'Swalpa tinniri' (please eat), 'Aaytu, dhanyavada' (enough, thank you), 'Tumba ruchi' (very tasty). Practice the exchange.
example: Host: 'Swalpa kaapi kudiyiri.' You: 'Dhanyavada, tumba chennagide.' (Thank you, it's very good.) This exchange builds real connection.

action: Describe your daily routine in Kannada. Write 8-10 sentences covering: waking up, morning routine, commute, work, evening, dinner, sleep. Use present tense.
description: Write your daily routine in Kannada. This combines vocabulary, grammar, and time words into a connected narrative.
context: Describing your routine is a classic language exercise because it uses high-frequency vocabulary and simple grammar in a natural sequence.
constraint_note: 8-10 sentences. Use time words (beligge, madhyahna, saanje, raatri). Use verbs you know. It's okay to mix Kannada and English for words you don't know yet.
example: Naanu beligge 7 gantege eddene. Kaapi kudiyuttene. Office-ge hoguttene. Saanje manege baruttene. Raatri 11 gantege malakuttene.

action: Learn numbers 11-20: Hannondu (11), Hannereradu (12), Hadimooru (13), Hadinaalku (14), Hadinaidu (15), Hadinaaru (16), Hadinelu (17), Hadinentu (18), Hattombattu (19), Ippattu (20).
description: Learn Kannada numbers 11-20. These follow a pattern: 'hadi' (ten) + unit number, with some irregularities.
context: Numbers 11-20 are essential for prices, addresses, and ages. Most market prices fall in this range.
constraint_note: Say them in sequence until fluent. Then practice random recall. Notice the pattern: hadi + number.
example: 11=Hannondu, 12=Hannereradu, 15=Hadinaidu, 20=Ippattu. Pattern breaks at 11, 12, 19, 20 — memorize those separately.

action: Learn number multiples: Ippattu (20), Movattu (30), Nalvattu (40), Aivattu (50), Aravattu (60), Eppattu (70), Embattu (80), Tombattu (90), Nooru (100).
description: Learn Kannada tens (20-100). With these + 1-10, you can say any number up to 100.
context: Tens let you handle any price or quantity. 'Aivattu rupaayi' (50 rupees), 'Movattu nimisha' (30 minutes).
constraint_note: Practice counting by tens: 10, 20, 30... 100. Then combine: 'Movattu aidu' (35), 'Aravattu mooru' (63).
example: 20=Ippattu, 50=Aivattu, 100=Nooru. Combine: Ippattu aidu=25, Aivattu mooru=53, Tombattu ombattu=99.

action: Learn 6 opinion phrases: 'Nanage ishta' (I like it), 'Nanage ishta illa' (I don't like it), 'Chennagide' (It's good), 'Tumba chennagide' (Very good), 'Sari illa' (Not okay), 'Nanage gothilla' (I don't know).
description: Learn to express opinions and preferences. These move you from transactional to conversational Kannada.
context: Expressing opinions is where personality enters language. 'Nanage ee cinema ishta' (I like this movie) is a real conversation, not a transaction.
constraint_note: Express 5 real opinions about things you like/dislike using these phrases. Say aloud.
example: ನನಗೆ ಇಷ್ಟ (Nanage ishta)=I like it. 'Nanage filter kaapi tumba ishta' = I really like filter coffee.

action: Try reading a Kannada shop sign, menu, or newspaper headline. Sound out each letter. Write down what you read and what you think it means.
description: Real-world reading practice. Find any Kannada text around you — a sign, a menu, a product label — and try to read it.
context: Reading real-world Kannada is the ultimate test of script knowledge. Even partial reading builds confidence enormously.
constraint_note: Find one piece of Kannada text. Sound out each letter. Write the transliteration. Guess the meaning. Look it up.
example: A shop sign might say ಹೋಟೆಲ್ (Hotel) or ಮೆಡಿಕಲ್ (Medical). Sound it out: ಹೋ=ho, ಟೆ=te, ಲ್=l → Hotel!

action: Learn imperative (command) forms: Baa/Banni (come), Hogu/Hogi (go), Kodu/Kodi (give), Nodu/Nodiri (look), Kootko/Kootkoli (sit). Informal vs formal pairs.
description: Learn command forms — informal and formal. You use these constantly: 'Kodi' (give), 'Banni' (come), 'Nodiri' (look).
context: Imperatives are among the most used verb forms. 'Ondu kaapi kodi' (give one coffee) uses the formal imperative — you'll say this daily.
constraint_note: Learn both informal (with friends) and formal (with strangers/elders) for each verb. Always default to formal.
example: Informal: ಬಾ (Baa)=come. Formal: ಬನ್ನಿ (Banni)=please come. Informal: ಕೊಡು (Kodu)=give. Formal: ಕೊಡಿ (Kodi)=please give.

action: Practice the Kannada nasal sounds: ಙ (nga), ಞ (nya), ಣ (Na), ನ (na), ಮ (ma). Say words containing each: ಅಂಗಡಿ (angadi), ಹಣ (haNa=money), ಮನೆ (mane).
description: Practice the 5 nasal consonants. Each is produced at a different position in the mouth — back to front.
context: Nasals are important in Kannada — many common words contain them. Getting the position right (especially ಣ vs ನ) improves your accent.
constraint_note: Say each nasal sound, then a word containing it. Feel where in your mouth the sound resonates.
example: ಮ (ma) = lips. ನ (na) = tongue on teeth. ಣ (Na) = tongue curled back. Three different 'n/m' sounds.

action: Learn 10 common adverbs: Tumba (very/much), Swalpa (a little), Bega (quickly), Nidhaana (slowly), Innoo (more/still), Yaavagalu (always), Kadime (less), Jaasti (more/too much), Saaku (enough), Matthe (again).
description: Learn 10 adverbs that modify verbs and adjectives. These add nuance to everything you say.
context: Adverbs transform basic sentences into natural speech. 'Bega banni' (come quickly) vs 'Nidhaanavaagi banni' (come slowly) — same verb, different urgency.
constraint_note: Add an adverb to 5 sentences you already know. Say the before and after versions aloud.
example: ತುಂಬಾ (Tumba)=very, ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ (Swalpa)=a little, ಬೇಗ (Bega)=quickly. 'Tumba chennagide' = Very good. 'Bega banni' = Come quickly.

items.body: Pronounced TUM-baa. Very/much — the most common intensifier. 'Tumba chennagide' = very good. 'Tumba dhanyavaada' = thank you very much. Used constantly in conversation. 'Tumba' can modify adjectives, verbs, and even other adverbs. Essential for expressing degree.

Pronounced SWAL-pa. A little/some. 'Swalpa neeru kodi' = give some water. 'Swalpa time' = a little time. Opposite of 'tumba.' 'Swalpa swalpa' = little by little. One of the most polite and useful words — adding 'swalpa' to any request makes it softer and more polite.

Pronounced BAY-ga. Quickly/fast. 'Bega baa' = come quickly. 'Bega maadu' = do it fast. 'Bega bega' = hurry hurry. Opposite is 'nidhaanakke' (slowly). In Bangalore traffic, 'bega hogakke aagalla' = can't go fast — a daily reality.

action: Write down 3 Kannada words or phrases you used (or tried to use) in real life this week. How did people respond?
description: Reflect on your real-world Kannada usage. What did you try? What worked? What felt awkward?
context: Tracking real usage connects learning to life. The words you actually use stick better than the ones you only practice alone.
constraint_note: Be honest. Even 'Namaskara' to your building watchman counts. Write what you said, to whom, and how they reacted.
example: Maybe you said 'Dhanyavada' to a delivery person, or 'Eshtu?' at a shop. Write it down. How did it feel?

action: Practice giving directions to your home in Kannada. Start from a landmark: '___ inda nere hogi, ___ hattira bala tiru, ___ nantara edakke tiru, ___ mane.'
description: Give directions to your home in Kannada. This combines place words, direction words, and landmarks into a practical skill.
context: Giving directions is a real test of spatial vocabulary. If you can direct an auto to your home in Kannada, your navigation vocabulary is solid.
constraint_note: Start from a landmark near your home. Use: nere hogi (go straight), bala/edakke tiru (turn right/left), hattira (near), nantara (after).
example: Silk Board-inda nere hogi, signal hattira bala tiru, Big Bazaar nantara edakke tiru, eradu nimisha nantara bala kade nanna mane.

action: From memory, write the present, past, and future tense conjugations for 'hogu' (go) — all pronouns. Then do the same for 'tinnu' (eat).
description: Full verb conjugation recall — 3 tenses × all pronouns × 2 verbs. This is the grammar stress test.
context: If you can conjugate two verbs across all tenses from memory, you've internalized the Kannada verb system. Everything else is just new roots.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write all forms. Check after. The forms you struggle with are your next focus.
example: hogu: hoguttene (I go), hogde (I went), hoguttene (I will go). tinnu: tinnuttene (I eat), tinde (I ate), tinnuttene (I will eat).

action: Learn 10 occupation words: Doctor, Teacher (adhyapaka/guru), Engineer, Driver, Farmer (raita), Shopkeeper (angadi-yavaru), Cook (aduge-yavaru), Student (vidyarthi), Police, Lawyer (vakila).
description: Learn 10 occupation words. These come up in introductions and conversations about people.
context: Occupation words help you talk about people: 'Avaru doctor' (They're a doctor), 'Nanna appa raita' (My father is a farmer).
constraint_note: Make 5 sentences about people you know using occupation words. Say aloud.
example: ವಿದ್ಯಾರ್ಥಿ (Vidyarthi)=student, ರೈತ (Raita)=farmer. 'Nanna anna engineer' = My elder brother is an engineer.

action: Learn to express obligation: 'beku' (must/should). 'Naanu hogbeku' (I must go), 'Neenu odabeku' (You should read), 'Kelsa maadabeku' (Must work).
description: Learn 'beku' for obligation — 'must' and 'should.' Different from 'beku' for want (which you already know).
context: 'Beku' does double duty in Kannada — it means both 'want' and 'must' depending on sentence structure. Context makes it clear.
constraint_note: Make 5 obligation sentences about things you need to do today. Say aloud. Notice how 'beku' attaches to the verb differently than when expressing want.
example: Want: ನನಗೆ ನೀರು ಬೇಕು (Nanage neeru beku)=I want water. Must: ನಾನು ಹೋಗಬೇಕು (Naanu hogbeku)=I must go. Different structure, same word.

action: Practice reading a full Kannada sentence in script: ನಾನು ಬೆಂಗಳೂರಿನಲ್ಲಿ ಕೆಲಸ ಮಾಡುತ್ತೇನೆ. Sound out each word, then read the whole sentence.
description: Read a complete Kannada sentence in script. This combines letter recognition, conjuncts, and vowel signs into real reading.
context: Reading a full sentence is a milestone. You're not just recognizing letters — you're reading Kannada.
constraint_note: Sound out each word separately first. Then read the whole sentence. Then say it naturally. Repeat 3 times.
example: ನಾನು=Naanu, ಬೆಂಗಳೂರಿನಲ್ಲಿ=Bengaloorinalli, ಕೆಲಸ=kelsa, ಮಾಡುತ್ತೇನೆ=maaduttene. = I work in Bangalore.

action: Practice the Kannada 'sh' sounds. There are two: ಶ (sha — palatal) and ಷ (Sha — retroflex). Say: ಶಾಲೆ (shaale=school), ಷಟ್ (shat=six in Sanskrit), ವಿಶೇಷ (vishesha=special).
description: Practice distinguishing the two 'sh' sounds in Kannada. Most learners merge them, but they're different.
context: ಶ is made with the tongue at the palate (like English 'sh'). ಷ is retroflex (tongue curled back). In practice, most Kannada speakers merge them, but knowing the difference helps reading.
constraint_note: Say each word 3 times. For ಷ, try curling your tongue back slightly while making the 'sh' sound.
example: ಶ (sha) = tongue at palate, like English 'sh'. ಷ (Sha) = tongue curled back. ಶಾಲೆ (shaale)=school, ಕೃಷ್ಣ (Krishna).

action: Learn 6 time-telling phrases: 'Eega eshtu gante?' (What time is it now?), 'Mooru gante' (3 o'clock), 'Mooru-a-vare' (3:30), 'Aidu nimisha' (5 minutes), 'Arda gante' (half hour), 'Hatthu nimisha-dalli baruttene' (I'll come in 10 minutes).
description: Learn to tell and ask about time in Kannada. Time phrases are essential for making plans and appointments.
context: Time-telling combines numbers with specific time vocabulary. 'Hatthu nimisha-dalli baruttene' (I'll come in 10 minutes) is something you'll say often.
constraint_note: Practice telling the current time in Kannada. Then make 3 time-based plans: 'Naale beligge hattu gantege sigona' (Shall we meet tomorrow morning at 10?).
example: ಈಗ ಎಷ್ಟು ಗಂಟೆ? (Eega eshtu gante?)=What time is it? ಮೂರು ಗಂಟೆ (Mooru gante)=3 o'clock.

action: Learn about the Kannada Rajyotsava (Karnataka Formation Day, Nov 1). Learn: 'Kannada Rajyotsava shubhashayagalu' (Happy Karnataka Day) and why it matters.
description: Learn about Karnataka's most important cultural celebration and the greeting used for it.
context: Kannada Rajyotsava is a matter of deep pride. Knowing about it and using the greeting shows cultural awareness and respect.
constraint_note: Learn the greeting, the date (November 1), and why it's celebrated (formation of Karnataka state in 1956). Use it next November 1.
example: ಕನ್ನಡ ರಾಜ್ಯೋತ್ಸವ ಶುಭಾಶಯಗಳು (Kannada Rajyotsava Shubhashayagalu) = Happy Karnataka Formation Day. Said on November 1.

action: Translate 5 simple English sentences to Kannada. Remember SOV order. 'I eat rice every day' → 'Naanu dina anna tinnuttene.'
description: Translation practice — convert English thoughts to Kannada. This trains you to think in Kannada word order.
context: Translation is a bridge skill. Eventually you want to think directly in Kannada, but translation builds the patterns first.
constraint_note: Translate these: 'I drink coffee in the morning.' 'She goes to office.' 'We like Bangalore.' 'He is my friend.' 'They came yesterday.'
example: I drink coffee in the morning → ನಾನು ಬೆಳಿಗ್ಗೆ ಕಾಫಿ ಕುಡಿಯುತ್ತೇನೆ (Naanu beligge kaapi kudiyuttene). SOV: I + morning + coffee + drink.

action: From memory, write all the polite phrases you know: please, sorry, thank you, excuse me, wait, say again, may I. Check yourself.
description: Recall all polite/courtesy phrases from memory. Politeness should be automatic — test if it is.
context: Polite phrases need to be reflexive. If you have to think about how to say 'sorry' or 'please,' it's not automatic enough yet.
constraint_note: Write every polite phrase you remember. Then check. The ones you forgot need more practice.
example: Dayavittu, Kshamisi, Dhanyavada, Swalpa kaali, Innomme heli... How many can you recall?

action: Learn 10 technology/modern words: Phone, Computer, Internet, WhatsApp, Photo, Video, Charge, Password, Download, Online. Most are borrowed — learn the Kannada pronunciation.
description: Learn how Kannada handles modern/tech words. Most are English borrowings with Kannada pronunciation and script.
context: Modern Kannada freely borrows English tech words but pronounces them differently. 'Phone' becomes 'Phon,' 'Computer' becomes 'Kampyootar.'
constraint_note: Write each in Kannada script. Practice the Kannada pronunciation. Make 3 sentences: 'Phone charge aagilla' (Phone isn't charged).
example: ಫೋನ್ (Phon), ಕಂಪ್ಯೂಟರ್ (Kampyootar). 'Phone charge maadi' = Charge the phone. 'Internet baruttilla' = Internet isn't coming/working.

action: Record yourself saying 5 Kannada sentences. Listen back. Compare with how you think they should sound. Note what feels unnatural.
description: Self-recording practice. Hearing yourself speak Kannada reveals pronunciation gaps you can't hear in real-time.
context: Recording yourself is uncomfortable but incredibly effective. You'll hear things you don't notice while speaking — speed, rhythm, specific sounds.
constraint_note: Record on your phone. Say 5 sentences you know well. Listen back. What sounds off? Focus on those sounds next time.
example: Record: 'Namaskara. Nanna hesaru ___. Naanu Bangalore-alli iddene. Nanage Kannada swalpa baratte. Dhanyavada.' Listen back critically.

action: Learn relative clauses: 'verb + a' creates 'who/which/that.' 'Bandavanu' (he who came), 'Naanu odida pustaka' (the book that I read).
description: Learn to form relative clauses — 'the person who came,' 'the food that I ate.' This is intermediate grammar that makes your Kannada sound natural.
context: Relative clauses let you describe things in context instead of choppy separate sentences. It's the bridge from basic to fluent-sounding Kannada.
constraint_note: Make 5 relative clause sentences about real things: 'Naanu ninne nodida cinema' (the movie I saw yesterday).
example: ನಾನು ಓದಿದ ಪುಸ್ತಕ (Naanu odida pustaka)=the book I read. ಅವನು ಮಾಡಿದ ಊಟ (Avanu maadida oota)=the food he made.

action: Practice a landlord/maintenance conversation: 'Neeru baruttilla' (water isn't coming), 'Light hogide' (power is out), 'Plumber kalisiri' (send a plumber), 'Yaavaga sari aagutte?' (When will it be fixed?), 'Rent kottiddene' (I've paid rent).
description: Learn housing/maintenance phrases. If you live in Bangalore, you'll need these with your landlord, watchman, or maintenance staff.
context: Housing interactions are often in Kannada even if your work is in English. These phrases handle 90% of maintenance conversations.
constraint_note: Role-play a complaint call: describe the problem, ask for help, ask when it'll be fixed. Say aloud.
example: ನೀರು ಬರುತ್ತಿಲ್ಲ (Neeru baruttilla)=Water isn't coming. ಯಾವಾಗ ಸರಿ ಆಗುತ್ತೆ? (Yaavaga sari aagutte?)=When will it be fixed?

action: Learn 10 action/activity words: Aata (play/game), Haadu (song/sing), Naatya (dance), Odu (read/run), Bareyu (write), Nidde (sleep), Snana (bath), Aduge (cooking), Kelsa (work), Prayana (travel).
description: Learn 10 activity words. These describe what people do — essential for talking about hobbies, routines, and plans.
context: Activity words let you talk about life beyond transactions: 'Nanage haadu keluvudu ishta' (I like listening to songs).
constraint_note: Make 5 sentences about activities you enjoy or do regularly. Use 'ishta' (like) or present tense.
example: ಆಟ (Aata)=game/play, ಹಾಡು (Haadu)=song. 'Nanage cricket aata ishta' = I like playing cricket.

action: Write a short paragraph in Kannada script (3-4 sentences) about yourself. Use transliteration as a guide if needed, but write in script.
description: Write connected Kannada text — not isolated words, but sentences that flow together. This is writing practice, not just letter practice.
context: Writing sentences in script builds fluency. Your hand learns the flow of connected Kannada, not just individual characters.
constraint_note: Write 3-4 sentences: your name, where you're from, what you do, one thing you like. In Kannada script.
example: ನನ್ನ ಹೆಸರು ___. ನಾನು ___-ಇಂದ ಬಂದಿದ್ದೇನೆ. ನಾನು ___-ಅಲ್ಲಿ ಕೆಲಸ ಮಾಡುತ್ತೇನೆ. ನನಗೆ ಕಾಫಿ ತುಂಬಾ ಇಷ್ಟ.

action: Learn to express 'want to do' vs 'have to do': 'maadabeku' (want to / have to do), 'maadona' (shall we do?), 'maadbahudu' (can do/may do).
description: Learn modal expressions — want to, have to, can, may, shall we. These express intention and possibility.
context: Modals are the difference between 'I go' and 'I want to go / I should go / shall we go?' They make your Kannada expressive.
constraint_note: Make 3 sentences with each modal: maadabeku (must), maadona (shall we?), maadbahudu (can/may).
example: ಹೋಗಬೇಕು (Hogbeku)=must go. ಹೋಗೋಣ (Hogona)=shall we go? ಹೋಗಬಹುದು (Hogbahudu)=can go/may go.

action: Practice Kannada word stress. Kannada generally stresses the first syllable. Say these words emphasizing the first syllable: BANgalore, KANnada, NAMaskara, CHENnagide.
description: Practice Kannada word stress patterns. Unlike English which varies stress, Kannada typically stresses the first syllable.
context: Correct stress makes your Kannada sound natural. English speakers often stress the wrong syllable, which sounds odd to native ears.
constraint_note: Say 10 Kannada words you know, emphasizing the first syllable each time. Notice how it changes the rhythm.
example: NAMaskara (not namasKAra), CHENnagide (not chennaGIde), BENgalooru (not bengaLOOru). First syllable gets the weight.

action: Learn about Kannada cinema (Sandalwood) basics: 5 famous actors/directors, the term 'Sandalwood,' and the phrase 'Ee cinema tumba chennagide' (This movie is very good).
description: Learn basic Kannada cinema vocabulary and cultural references. Movies are a huge part of Karnataka culture.
context: Kannada cinema (Sandalwood) is a point of pride. Knowing a few names and being able to discuss a movie in Kannada builds cultural connection.
constraint_note: Learn: Sandalwood = Kannada film industry. Learn 3 phrases: 'Ee cinema chennagide,' 'Yaaru hero?,' 'Naanu ee cinema nodiddene.'
example: 'Ee cinema tumba chennagide' = This movie is very good. 'KGF nodiddira?' = Have you seen KGF? Cinema is a universal conversation starter.

action: Speed round: write as many Kannada words as you can in 5 minutes. Any category, any word. Just dump everything you know.
description: Total vocabulary dump — write every Kannada word you know in 5 minutes. This measures your total active vocabulary.
context: Your total word count is a rough measure of progress. 50 words = survival. 100 = basic conversation. 200+ = you're getting somewhere.
constraint_note: Set a 5-minute timer. Write as fast as you can. Don't organize — just dump. Count at the end.
example: Namaskara, neeru, anna, kaapi, mane, hogu, baa, tumba, swalpa, beku, beda... How many can you get?

action: Practice small talk: 'Yelli kelsa?' (Where do you work?), 'Eshtu varsha aaytu Bangalore-alli?' (How many years in Bangalore?), 'Moola yelli?' (Where are you originally from?), 'Weekend enu plan?' (What's the weekend plan?).
description: Learn small talk phrases. These are the conversations that happen with colleagues, neighbours, and acquaintances.
context: Small talk in Kannada transforms relationships. Your watchman, your colleague, your chai wallah — a few phrases in their language changes everything.
constraint_note: Practice a 2-minute small talk conversation with an imaginary colleague. Include: greeting, asking about work, weekend plans, goodbye.
example: Hegiddira? Kelsa hege nadeyuttide? Weekend enu plan? Sari, nodona. Hogi banni! — A complete small talk exchange.

action: Learn to read Kannada numbers in script: ೧೨೩೪೫೬೭೮೯೦. Practice reading prices: ₹೫೦, ₹೧೨೫, ₹೨೫೦.
description: Learn Kannada numerals. While Arabic numerals are common, Kannada numerals appear on buses, government signs, and traditional contexts.
context: Kannada numerals on bus route numbers are the most practical use. If you can read ೩೦ on a bus, you know it's route 30.
constraint_note: Write each numeral 0-9. Practice reading: ೧೫ (15), ೩೦ (30), ೫೦ (50), ೧೦೦ (100). Look for them on buses next time.

action: Learn passive voice: 'maadalpattitu' (was done). 'Baagilu muchalpattitu' (Door was closed). Practice 5 passive sentences.
description: Learn passive construction in Kannada. Passive voice is used in formal speech and news.
context: Passive voice appears in news, announcements, and formal Kannada. Recognizing it helps comprehension even if you don't use it actively.
constraint_note: Convert 5 active sentences to passive. Say both versions aloud.
example: Active: ಅವನು ಬಾಗಿಲು ಮುಚ್ಚಿದ (He closed the door). Passive: ಬಾಗಿಲು ಮುಚ್ಚಲ್ಪಟ್ಟಿತು (The door was closed).

action: Learn 10 kitchen/cooking words: Aduge mane (kitchen), Paatra (vessel), Sottu (ladle), Chaaqu (knife), Oole (stove), Enne (oil), Uppu (salt), Sakkare (sugar), Haal (milk), Beesuvkallu (grinding stone).
description: Learn kitchen vocabulary. Useful for home conversations and understanding recipes.
context: Kitchen words connect you to domestic life in Kannada. 'Swalpa uppu haaki' (add a little salt) is everyday home language.
constraint_note: Make 3 cooking instructions: 'Enne haaki' (add oil), 'Uppu swalpa haaki' (add a little salt), 'Bisi maadi' (make it hot).
example: ಉಪ್ಪು (Uppu)=salt, ಸಕ್ಕರೆ (Sakkare)=sugar, ಎಣ್ಣೆ (Enne)=oil. 'Kaapi-ge sakkare beda' = No sugar in coffee.

items.body: Pronounced UP-pu. Salt. 'Uppu haaku' = add salt. 'Uppu jaasti' = too much salt. 'Uppu illade' = without salt. A basic kitchen word used daily. The idiom 'uppu tinda mele neeru kudibeku' = after eating salt, drink water (face consequences).

Pronounced SAK-ka-re. Sugar. 'Sakkare haaku' = add sugar. 'Sakkare kaafi' = sugar coffee. 'Sakkare illade' = without sugar. In Karnataka, jaggery ('bella') is often preferred over refined sugar. 'Sakkare khaayle' = sugar disease (diabetes).

Pronounced EN-ne. Oil. 'Enne haaku' = add oil. 'Kobbari enne' = coconut oil (most common in Karnataka). 'Enne snana' = oil bath (a weekly tradition of applying oil before bathing). 'Enne' is essential for cooking — 'enne illade aduge illa' = no cooking without oil.

action: Practice a phone call to order food delivery: 'Namaskara, order maadabeku' → items → address → 'Eshtu time aagutte?' → 'Dhanyavada.'
description: Role-play ordering food delivery in Kannada. A practical scenario you can actually use.
context: Food delivery calls are a safe, real practice opportunity. The interaction is predictable and the stakes are low.
constraint_note: Say the full call aloud: greeting, order 2 items, give your address, ask delivery time, thank them.
example: Namaskara, ondu biryani mattu ondu gobi manchurian. Address: ___. Eshtu time aagutte? Sari, dhanyavada.

action: Learn about Ugadi (Kannada New Year). Learn: 'Ugadi shubhashayagalu' (Happy Ugadi), Bevu-Bella tradition (neem+jaggery = life's bitter and sweet), and Panchanga Shravana.
description: Learn about Ugadi — the Kannada/Telugu New Year. One of the most important festivals in Karnataka.
context: Ugadi is deeply significant. The Bevu-Bella tradition (eating neem and jaggery together) symbolizes accepting life's bitter and sweet moments.
constraint_note: Learn the greeting, the Bevu-Bella meaning, and when it falls (March/April). Use the greeting next Ugadi.
example: ಉಗಾದಿ ಶುಭಾಶಯಗಳು (Ugadi Shubhashayagalu)=Happy Ugadi. Bevu=neem (bitter), Bella=jaggery (sweet). Life has both.

action: From memory, write one sentence each using: present tense, past tense, future tense, continuous tense, conditional, negative, and question form.
description: Grammar recall: produce one sentence in each grammatical form you've learned. Tests whether grammar is usable, not just recognized.
context: If you can produce all 7 sentence types from memory, your grammar foundation is solid. The ones you struggle with need more practice.
constraint_note: 7 sentences, 7 different forms. No peeking. Write first, check after.
example: Present: Naanu hoguttene. Past: Naanu hogde. Future: Naanu hoguttene. Continuous: Naanu hoguttiddene. Conditional: Mazhe bandre... Negative: Naanu hogalla. Question: Neenu hoguttiya?

action: Read this Kannada paragraph and write the transliteration: ನಾನು ಇವತ್ತು ಬೆಳಿಗ್ಗೆ ಕಾಫಿ ಕುಡಿದೆ. ನಂತರ ಆಫೀಸ್‌ಗೆ ಹೋದೆ. ಸಂಜೆ ಮನೆಗೆ ಬಂದೆ.
description: Read a 3-sentence Kannada paragraph and transliterate it. Tests connected reading ability.
context: Reading connected text is harder than isolated words. Your eyes need to learn where words begin and end in Kannada script.
constraint_note: Read each word, write the transliteration, then read the whole paragraph aloud smoothly.
example: ನಾನು=Naanu, ಇವತ್ತು=ivattu, ಬೆಳಿಗ್ಗೆ=beligge, ಕಾಫಿ=kaapi, ಕುಡಿದೆ=kudide. = I drank coffee this morning.

action: Learn 10 relationship/social words: Snehita (friend), Nentaru (relative), Pakka-mane (neighbour), Yajamana (boss/owner), Giraaki (customer), Athithi (guest), Oota (meal/food), Maduve (wedding), Habba (festival), Samaachara (news).
description: Learn 10 social/relationship words. These come up in conversations about people and events.
context: Social vocabulary lets you participate in conversations about life events — weddings, festivals, visitors. This is where language becomes community.
constraint_note: Make 5 sentences: 'Nanna snehita bandiddane' (My friend has come), 'Naale habba' (Tomorrow is a festival).
example: ಸ್ನೇಹಿತ (Snehita)=friend, ಮದುವೆ (Maduve)=wedding, ಹಬ್ಬ (Habba)=festival. 'Naale nanna snehitana maduve' = Tomorrow is my friend's wedding.

items.body: Pronounced SNAY-hi-ta. Friend. 'Nanna snehita' = my friend. More formal than 'guru' or 'machha' (informal buddy terms). 'Snehita-ru' = friends (plural). 'Sneha' = friendship/affection. In formal introductions: 'ivaru nanna snehitaru' = this is my friend.

Pronounced MA-du-ve. Wedding/marriage. 'Maduve aagide' = got married. 'Maduve-ge banni' = come to the wedding. Karnataka weddings are elaborate multi-day affairs. 'Maduve mantapa' = wedding hall. 'Maduve oota' = wedding feast — often the highlight for guests.

Pronounced HUB-ba. Festival. 'Habba bandide' = festival has come. Karnataka celebrates many festivals: Dasara, Ugadi, Sankranti. 'Habba habba' = festive atmosphere. 'Habba oota' = festival meal (special dishes). 'Habbada shubhaashaya' = festival greetings.

action: Learn reported speech: 'Avanu hoguttene anda' (He said he'll go). 'Avalu baruttale andu' (She said she'll come). Convert 5 direct quotes to reported speech.
description: Learn indirect/reported speech — 'he said that...' This is how you relay conversations and messages.
context: Reported speech is essential for relaying messages: 'Boss naale meeting ide andru' (Boss said there's a meeting tomorrow).
constraint_note: Convert 5 direct statements to reported speech. Say both versions aloud.
example: Direct: 'Naanu baruttene' (I'll come). Reported: Avanu baruttene anda (He said he'll come).

action: Practice the Kannada 'e' and 'o' vowel pairs. Short vs long: ಎ (e) vs ಏ (ee), ಒ (o) vs ಓ (oo). Say: ಎಲೆ (ele=leaf) vs ಏಳು (elu=seven), ಒಳ (ola=inside) vs ಓಡು (odu=run).
description: Practice short vs long e/o vowels. These pairs are commonly confused by learners.
context: Short ಎ and long ಏ sound similar but are different phonemes. Getting them right improves both pronunciation and reading.
constraint_note: Say each pair 5 times. Hold the long vowel noticeably longer. Feel the difference.
example: ಎಲೆ (ele)=leaf (short e), ಏಳು (elu)=seven (long e). ಒಳ (ola)=inside (short o), ಓಡು (odu)=run (long o).

action: Learn 5 common Kannada blessings and wishes: 'Shubhavagali' (May it be auspicious), 'Devaru olledu maadli' (May God do good), 'Aayushya vriddhi aagali' (May your life be long), 'Yeshasviyagali' (May you succeed).
description: Learn Kannada blessings used at weddings, festivals, and when meeting elders. Blessings are a core part of Kannada social culture.
context: Elders bless younger people in Kannada culture. Knowing these phrases shows deep cultural respect and earns genuine warmth.
constraint_note: Learn when each is used: weddings, birthdays, new ventures, meeting elders. Practice saying each with warmth.
example: ಶುಭವಾಗಲಿ (Shubhavagali)=May it be auspicious — used at weddings, new beginnings. ಯಶಸ್ವಿಯಾಗಲಿ (Yeshasviyagali)=May you succeed — for exams, new jobs.

action: Practice asking for and understanding directions: 'Majestic-ge hege hogbeku?' Then understand: 'Nere hogi, signal-alli bala tiru, 500 meter nantara edakke ide.'
description: Practice both giving AND receiving directions. Understanding spoken directions is harder than giving them.
context: Receiving directions tests listening comprehension. In real life, people speak fast — practice parsing direction sequences.
constraint_note: Write directions to 3 places you know. Then read them aloud as if someone asked you. Practice both roles.
example: Q: 'MG Road-ge hege hogbeku?' A: 'Illi-inda nere hogi, eradu signal nantara bala tiru, metro station hattira ide.'

action: Learn 10 nature/geography words specific to Karnataka: Kaadu (forest), Jalapaata (waterfall), Samudra (sea/ocean), Gudda (hill), Gadde (field), Tota (garden), Kola (pond), Hola (stream), Malenaadu (hill country), Bayaluseeme (plains).
description: Learn Karnataka geography vocabulary. Karnataka has diverse landscapes — coast, hills, plains — each with specific vocabulary.
context: Karnataka geography words come up in travel conversations: 'Coorg-alli tumba kaadu ide' (Coorg has lots of forests). Useful for weekend trip planning.
constraint_note: Make 3 sentences about Karnataka places: 'Jog Falls doddu jalapaata' (Jog Falls is a big waterfall).
example: ಕಾಡು (Kaadu)=forest, ಜಲಪಾತ (Jalapaata)=waterfall, ಮಲೆನಾಡು (Malenaadu)=hill country (Western Ghats region).

items.body: Pronounced KAA-du. Forest/jungle. 'Kaadu praani' = wild animal. Karnataka has rich forests in the Western Ghats. 'Kaadu-mele' = on the forest/hill. 'Kaadina madhya' = in the middle of the forest. Nagarhole and Bandipur are famous 'kaadu' areas in Karnataka.

Pronounced JA-la-PAA-ta. Waterfall — literally 'water-fall.' 'Jala' = water, 'paata' = fall. Karnataka has stunning waterfalls: Jog Falls (Jogada Jalapaata) is India's second highest. 'Jalapaata nodakke hogona' = shall we go see the waterfall? A useful word for tourism.

Pronounced MA-le-NAA-du. Hill country — specifically the Western Ghats region of Karnataka. 'Male' = rain/hill, 'naadu' = land/country. Malnad is known for coffee plantations, dense forests, and heavy rainfall. 'Malenaadina haava' = Malnad climate. It's both a geographic term and a cultural identity — Malnad people have distinct food, customs, and dialect.

action: Learn to express likes/dislikes with reasons: 'Nanage ___ ishta yaakandre ___' (I like ___ because ___). Make 5 sentences with reasons.
description: Express preferences with reasons. This combines opinion phrases with 'because' — a big step toward real conversation.
context: Giving reasons makes your opinions interesting. 'Nanage Bangalore ishta yaakandre havaamana chennagide' (I like Bangalore because the weather is good).
constraint_note: 5 sentences: what you like/dislike + why. Use 'yaakandre' (because).
example: ನನಗೆ filter kaapi ಇಷ್ಟ ಯಾಕಂದ್ರೆ tumba ruchi (I like filter coffee because it's very tasty).

action: Write a complete self-introduction in Kannada from memory: name, origin, work, how long in Bangalore, what you like about Karnataka, one hobby. 6+ sentences.
description: Full self-introduction from memory. This is your Kannada elevator pitch — it should flow naturally.
context: If you can introduce yourself fluently in Kannada, you've crossed a major threshold. This is the single most useful thing to have memorized.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write it out. Then say it aloud 3 times. Time yourself — aim for under 30 seconds.
example: Namaskara. Nanna hesaru ___. Naanu ___-inda. ___ varsha-inda Bangalore-alli iddene. Nanage Karnataka tumba ishta. Nanage ___ ishta.

action: Practice writing 5 complete sentences in Kannada script from dictation. Have someone read these aloud (or read the transliteration yourself) and write in script.
description: Dictation practice — hear Kannada, write in script. This tests the full loop: listening → processing → writing.
context: Dictation combines listening and writing skills. It's one of the most effective exercises for script internalization.
constraint_note: Read each transliteration aloud, then write in Kannada script without looking at the script version. Check after.
example: Dictate to yourself: 'Naanu ivattu office-ge hogde' → write: ನಾನು ಇವತ್ತು ಆಫೀಸ್‌ಗೆ ಹೋದೆ. Check your script.

action: Read a Kannada tongue twister aloud 5 times, getting faster each time: 'Kaage kaage kaakana koogige kaage kaakana koogi' (Crow crow, crow's call is crow crow's call).
description: Tongue twister practice. Tongue twisters train mouth muscles for sounds that don't exist in your native language.
context: Tongue twisters are fun and effective. They force your mouth to make rapid Kannada sound transitions that normal speech doesn't demand.
constraint_note: Start slow. Get each sound right. Then speed up gradually over 5 repetitions. Laugh when you mess up — that's the point.
example: Start: Kaa-ge kaa-ge kaa-ka-na koo-gi-ge... Speed up each round. By round 5, try to say it at conversational speed.

action: Learn 10 common Kannada-English code-switch words that Bangaloreans use: 'Adjust maadi' (adjust/manage), 'Plan maadu' (make a plan), 'Try maadu' (try), 'Use maadu' (use), 'Start maadu' (start).
description: Learn Kannada-English hybrid phrases. Real Bangalore Kannada mixes English verbs with Kannada grammar — this IS how people speak.
context: Purists may disagree, but code-switching is real Bangalore Kannada. 'Meeting attend maadidya?' is how your colleagues actually talk.
constraint_note: Make 5 hybrid sentences you'd actually use at work or with friends. This is practical, not textbook.
example: 'Meeting attend maadidya?' (Did you attend the meeting?), 'Swalpa adjust maadi' (Please adjust a little), 'Plan enu?' (What's the plan?)

action: Learn to express time duration: '___ inda' (since/from), '___ varege' (until). 'Monday-inda Friday varege kelsa' (Work from Monday to Friday). Make 5 duration sentences.
description: Learn to express time ranges — from/since and until. Essential for schedules, plans, and describing routines.
context: Duration expressions make your time vocabulary functional: 'Eradu varsha-inda Bangalore-alli iddene' (I've been in Bangalore for 2 years).
constraint_note: Make 5 sentences using inda/varege about your real schedule and life.
example: ಎರಡು ವರ್ಷದಿಂದ (Eradu varsha-inda)=since 2 years. ಸಂಜೆ ವರೆಗೆ (Saanje varege)=until evening.

action: Write about one thing that surprised you about Karnataka culture since you moved here. Write it in a mix of Kannada and English — use whatever Kannada you can.
description: Reflect on cultural observations using whatever Kannada you have. Mixing languages is fine — it's how real learning works.
context: Cultural reflection deepens your connection to the language. Language isn't just words — it's the world those words describe.
constraint_note: Write 3-4 sentences. Use as much Kannada as you can. Fill gaps with English. No pressure to be perfect.
example: 'Nanage surprise aagiddu — Bangalore-alli everyone says 'saar' and 'madam' tumba respectfully. Hindi-alli idu illa.' — Mix freely.

action: Practice a complete shopping interaction at a clothing store: browsing, asking size/color, trying on, negotiating price, paying. All in Kannada.
description: Role-play a clothing shop visit. This combines vocabulary (clothes, colors, numbers) with phrases (asking, negotiating, paying).
context: Clothing shopping is a longer interaction than buying vegetables — it tests sustained Kannada conversation over multiple exchanges.
constraint_note: Full interaction aloud: enter, browse, ask about an item, ask for different size/color, ask price, negotiate, decide, pay.
example: Ee shirt bere size-alli ide? Large beku. Bere banna? Neeli ide? Eshtu? Swalpa kammi maadi. Sari, card swipe maadbahudu?

action: Learn 10 common verbs (set 2): Kootko (sit), Nillu (stand/stop), Malako (sleep/lie down), Eddu (get up), Togoli (wash), Haaku (put/pour), Tegidu (take/remove), Ittu (keep/place), Hididu (hold/catch), Beesu (sweep/fan).
description: Learn 10 more everyday verbs. These cover domestic and physical actions you do daily.
context: These verbs fill gaps in daily description: 'Kootko' (sit), 'Eddu' (get up), 'Malako' (lie down) — you use these actions every day.
constraint_note: Make a sentence with each verb. Say aloud: 'Illi kootko' (sit here), 'Bega eddu' (get up quickly).
example: ಕೂತ್ಕೊ (Kootko)=sit, ನಿಲ್ಲು (Nillu)=stop/stand, ಮಲಕೊ (Malako)=lie down. 'Swalpa illi kootko' = Sit here for a bit.

items.body: Pronounced KOOT-ko. Sit (informal). 'Illi kootko' = sit here. The formal version is 'kootkolri' or 'kootkondi.' 'Kootko' is used with friends and children. In Karnataka hospitality, the first thing you say to a guest is 'banni, kootko' = come, sit down. Sitting is the beginning of every social interaction.

Pronounced NIL-lu. Stop/stand. 'Illi nillu' = stop here (useful for auto-rickshaws). 'Nillu, nillu!' = stop, stop! 'Nillisu' = make it stop. Dual meaning of stop and stand: 'ninta nodu' = stand and look. One of the most practical verbs for navigation and daily commands.

Pronounced MA-la-ko. Lie down/sleep (informal). 'Hogi malako' = go lie down. 'Malako' is casual; 'malakkondi' is more respectful. 'Malkondu idya?' = are you lying down/sleeping? Used for both the act of lying down and sleeping. 'Nidde malako' = sleep (literally 'sleep lie-down').

action: Find a Kannada text on your phone (WhatsApp message, news headline, app notification). Try to read it. Write down what you understood.
description: Real digital reading practice. Kannada text on screens is everywhere — WhatsApp, notifications, Google Maps.
context: Digital Kannada is the most accessible reading material. You don't need a book — your phone has Kannada text right now.
constraint_note: Find one piece of Kannada text on your phone. Sound out each word. Write transliteration. Guess meaning.
example: A WhatsApp forward, a Google Maps label in Kannada, a notification from Swiggy/Zomato in Kannada. Try to decode it.

action: Learn to express wishes and hypotheticals: 'Naanu Kannada chennagi maataadidre chennagittu' (It would be nice if I spoke Kannada well). Pattern: verb + idre + result + ittu.
description: Learn hypothetical/wish sentences — 'if only,' 'it would be nice if.' This is advanced grammar that expresses desires and regrets.
context: Hypotheticals let you dream in Kannada: 'Nanage raje sigidre Coorg-ge hoguttidde' (If I got leave, I'd go to Coorg).
constraint_note: Make 3 wish sentences about real things you want. Say aloud.
example: ನನಗೆ ರಜೆ ಸಿಕ್ಕಿದ್ರೆ Coorg-ಗೆ ಹೋಗುತ್ತಿದ್ದೆ (If I got leave, I'd go to Coorg).

action: Write a Kannada shopping list for a week's groceries. Include at least 15 items in Kannada. Use quantities: 'Eradu kg aalu, ondu packet haal, aidu eerulli.'
description: Write a real grocery list in Kannada. This tests practical vocabulary — can you name what you actually buy?
context: A grocery list is the most practical vocabulary test. If you can write your weekly shopping in Kannada, your food/household vocabulary is solid.
constraint_note: 15+ items with quantities. Write in Kannada (script or transliteration). Include: vegetables, fruits, dairy, staples.
example: Eradu kg anna, ondu kg aalu, ardhaa kg eerulli, ondu packet haal, mooru baalehannu, swalpa mensinakayi...

action: Practice the Kannada 'au' diphthong: ಔ. Say: ಔಷಧ (aushadha=medicine), ಕೌಶಲ (kaushala=skill), ಔತಣ (autana=feast). Compare with ಓ (long o).
description: Practice the 'au' vowel sound — a diphthong that glides from 'a' to 'u'. It's different from the long 'o' (ಓ).
context: The 'au' diphthong appears in many Sanskrit-origin words used in formal Kannada. Getting it right helps with formal vocabulary.
constraint_note: Say each word 3 times. Feel the glide from 'a' to 'u'. Compare: ಓಡು (odu=run) vs ಔಷಧ (aushadha=medicine).
example: ಔ starts as 'a' and glides to 'u' — like 'ow' in 'how'. ಔಷಧ (aushadha)=medicine. ಔತಣ (autana)=feast.

action: Learn 6 agreement/disagreement phrases: 'Sari' (okay/right), 'Haudu' (yes), 'Illa' (no), 'Nija' (true), 'Sari alla' (not right), 'Nanage ondu abhipraaya ide' (I have an opinion).
description: Learn to agree and disagree in Kannada. These are conversational essentials.
context: Agreement/disagreement phrases are the backbone of conversation. 'Haudu, nija' (yes, true) or 'Illa, sari alla' (no, that's not right) — you need these constantly.
constraint_note: Practice responding to imaginary statements with agreement or disagreement. Vary your responses.
example: ಹೌದು (Haudu)=yes, ಇಲ್ಲ (Illa)=no, ಸರಿ (Sari)=okay/correct, ನಿಜ (Nija)=true. 'Haudu, nija heltiddira' = Yes, you're saying the truth.

action: Write a short email/message in Kannada to an imaginary Kannada-speaking colleague: greeting, one request, one piece of information, closing. 4-5 sentences.
description: Write a Kannada message — the kind you'd send on WhatsApp to a colleague or neighbour. Practical written communication.
context: Written Kannada (messages, WhatsApp) is increasingly common. Being able to write a simple message is a practical milestone.
constraint_note: 4-5 sentences. Include: greeting, purpose, one detail, closing. It's okay to mix English for words you don't know.
example: Namaskara. Naale meeting 3 gantege ide. Dayavittu banni. Presentation ready maadi. Dhanyavada.

action: Learn 5 Kannada food-specific terms: Oota (meal), Tindi (snack), Uppittu (upma), Avalakki (poha/flattened rice), Ragi mudde (ragi ball). These are Karnataka staples.
description: Learn Karnataka-specific food terms. These aren't just translations — they're foods unique to Karnataka cuisine.
context: Karnataka has distinct food culture. Knowing 'ragi mudde,' 'bisi bele bath,' 'uppittu' shows you've engaged with local food, not just restaurant menus.
constraint_note: Learn each food, what it is, and when it's eaten. Try ordering one at a local restaurant this week.
example: ರಾಗಿ ಮುದ್ದೆ (Ragi mudde)=ragi ball (staple in North Karnataka), ಅವಲಕ್ಕಿ (Avalakki)=poha/flattened rice, ಉಪ್ಪಿಟ್ಟು (Uppittu)=upma.

action: Write the Kannada alphabet from memory: all 14 vowels, then all 34 consonants. Time yourself. Check and note gaps.
description: Full alphabet recall — the ultimate script test. If you can write the complete alphabet from memory, you own the script.
context: This is a milestone test. Don't worry about getting 100% — even 80% means you've internalized the script. Focus on the gaps.
constraint_note: Vowels first (14), then consonants in order (5 rows of 5 + 9 additional). Time yourself. Check after.
example: Vowels: ಅ ಆ ಇ ಈ ಉ ಊ ಋ ಎ ಏ ಐ ಒ ಓ ಔ + ಅಂ ಅಃ. Consonants: ಕ ಖ ಗ ಘ ಙ | ಚ ಛ ಜ ಝ ಞ | ...

action: Practice a complete bank/post office interaction: 'Nanage account open maadabeku' (I need to open an account), 'ID proof ide' (I have ID proof), 'Form kodi' (give the form), 'Sign yelli maadabeku?' (Where to sign?).
description: Learn formal/official interaction phrases. Banks, post offices, and government offices often operate in Kannada.
context: Official interactions in Karnataka often default to Kannada. Having basic phrases ready prevents the awkward 'English please' moment.
constraint_note: Role-play a bank visit: enter, state purpose, provide documents, ask questions, thank. Say aloud.
example: Namaskara. Nanage savings account open maadabeku. ID proof — Aadhaar card ide. Form kodi. Sign yelli? Dhanyavada.

action: Learn possessive forms: Nanna (my), Ninna (your-informal), Nimma (your-formal), Avana (his), Avala (her), Namma (our), Avara (their). Make 5 possessive phrases.
description: Learn possessive pronouns — my, your, his, her, our, their. These are used in almost every conversation.
context: Possessives are high-frequency words: 'Nanna mane' (my house), 'Nimma hesaru enu?' (What's your name?). You'll use these constantly.
constraint_note: Make 5 phrases: 'Nanna phone' (my phone), 'Nimma mane' (your house), 'Avana car' (his car).
example: ನನ್ನ (Nanna)=my, ನಿಮ್ಮ (Nimma)=your (formal), ನಮ್ಮ (Namma)=our. 'Namma Bengalooru' = Our Bangalore — a phrase of pride.

action: Practice aspirated vs unaspirated stops in real words: ಪಾಲು (paalu=milk) vs ಫಲ (phala=fruit), ಬಾಗಿಲು (baagilu=door) vs ಭಾರತ (bhaarata=India).
description: Practice aspirated consonant pairs in real words. The aspiration distinction carries meaning.
context: Aspiration errors are the most common pronunciation mistake for English speakers learning Kannada. This drill targets the specific pairs.
constraint_note: Say each pair 5 times. Feel the air puff on the aspirated version. Hold your hand in front of your mouth to check.
example: ಪ (pa) no puff → ಫ (pha) puff. ಬ (ba) no puff → ಭ (bha) puff. ತ (ta) no puff → ಥ (tha) puff.

action: Describe your home in Kannada: rooms, furniture, what's in each room. Write 10+ sentences using household, color, and location vocabulary.
description: Describe your living space in Kannada. This combines household vocabulary, colors, postpositions, and adjectives into connected description.
context: Describing your home tests whether vocabulary categories connect. You need household words + colors + postpositions + adjectives all working together.
constraint_note: 10+ sentences. Include: rooms, furniture, colors, locations (mele, kelage, olage). Use as much Kannada as you can.
example: Nanna mane-yalli eradu rooms ide. Kitchen chikku. Bedroom-alli doddu haasi ide. Kitiki hattira ondu kurchi ide.

action: Learn about the concept of 'Atithi Devo Bhava' in Karnataka — guest is God. Learn phrases: 'Banni, kootko' (Come, sit), 'Kaapi/Neeru beku?' (Want coffee/water?), 'Oota aaytu?' (Have you eaten?).
description: Understand Karnataka hospitality culture. Offering food/drink to guests is not optional — it's a deep cultural norm.
context: 'Oota aaytu?' (Have you eaten?) is not a question — it's an invitation. Understanding this cultural layer makes you a better guest and host in Karnataka.
constraint_note: Learn the hospitality sequence: invite in, offer seat, offer drink, offer food. Practice the phrases.
example: Banni, olage banni. Kootko. Kaapi beku? Oota aaytu? — This sequence IS Karnataka hospitality.

action: Write a paragraph about your last weekend in Kannada. 6-8 sentences using past tense. Where did you go? What did you eat? Who did you meet?
description: Write about a real past event in Kannada. This tests past tense, vocabulary, and connected narrative all at once.
context: Narrating past events is a core conversational skill. 'Weekend enu maadidya?' (What did you do this weekend?) is a question you'll hear every Monday.
constraint_note: 6-8 sentences in past tense. Be specific: places, food, people. Mix English for words you don't know.
example: Shanichaara naanu Lalbagh-ge hogde. Tumba sundara ittu. Coffee kudide. Snehitana jote oota maadide. Raatri manege bande.

action: Practice a conversation with your building watchman/security: greeting, asking about packages, reporting a visitor, asking about water/power schedule. All in Kannada.
description: Role-play a watchman interaction. This is one of the most common daily Kannada conversations for apartment dwellers in Bangalore.
context: Your building watchman likely speaks Kannada as a first language. Even basic Kannada with them builds a real relationship.
constraint_note: Full interaction: greeting, ask about a delivery, tell them a friend is coming, ask about water timing. Say aloud.
example: Namaskara. Ivattu yaavadu parcel bandide? Nanna snehita baruttane, olage bidiri. Neeru yaavaga baratte? Dhanyavada.

action: Learn 10 common Kannada exclamations: Aiyyo (oh no!), Devare (oh God!), Shabaash (well done!), Saaaku (enough!), Hogi (go away!), Nodu (look!), Keliri (listen!), Aashcharya (amazing!), Chee (ugh/disgust), Abba (phew/relief).
description: Learn Kannada exclamations — the emotional punctuation of speech. These make your Kannada sound alive, not robotic.
context: Exclamations are pure emotion. 'Aiyyo!' when something goes wrong, 'Shabaash!' when something goes right — these are the sounds of real Kannada.
constraint_note: Say each with the right emotion. Exaggerate. These should be felt, not just pronounced.
example: ಅಯ್ಯೋ (Aiyyo!)=oh no!, ದೇವರೇ (Devare!)=oh God!, ಶಾಬಾಶ್ (Shabaash!)=well done! Say them with feeling.

action: Translate these 5 English paragraphs to Kannada (1-2 sentences each): introduce a friend, describe today's weather, explain why you're late, order food for two people, give directions to your office.
description: Multi-scenario translation test. Each mini-paragraph uses different vocabulary and grammar — tests your range.
context: Real Kannada usage jumps between contexts constantly. This tests whether you can switch between social, descriptive, transactional, and directional Kannada.
constraint_note: 5 mini-paragraphs, each 1-2 sentences. Write in Kannada. Check what you couldn't translate — those are your gaps.
example: 1. 'This is my friend Rahul. He works at Google.' → Ivanu nanna snehita Rahul. Avanu Google-alli kelsa maaduttane.

action: Write a WhatsApp message in Kannada script to an imaginary friend: invite them for coffee this weekend. Include: greeting, invitation, place, time, closing.
description: Write a real-format Kannada message. WhatsApp messages are the most common form of written Kannada today.
context: If you can write a WhatsApp message in Kannada, you can communicate in written Kannada. This is the practical writing milestone.
constraint_note: Write in Kannada script (or transliteration if script is too slow). 3-4 sentences. Make it natural, not formal.
example: ಹೇ! ಈ weekend kaapi kudiyona? Saturday beligge 11 gantege? Third Wave Coffee, Koramangala. Helu!

action: Write about your Kannada learning journey so far. What's been easiest? Hardest? Most surprising? What motivates you to continue? Write in a mix of Kannada and English.
description: Reflect on your learning journey. What's working? What's not? What do you want to focus on next?
context: Periodic reflection keeps learning intentional. Knowing what's hard helps you focus. Knowing what's easy builds confidence.
constraint_note: Be honest. Write 4-5 sentences. Use Kannada where you can. This is for you, not for anyone else.
example: Script was hardest — but now naanu swalpa odabahudu. Pronunciation is getting better. Nanage auto-alli Kannada maataadodu tumba ishta. Grammar is still katina.

action: Read this short story and learn the highlighted Kannada words in context.
description: A short story about Ramu's morning at a chai stall, with Kannada vocabulary embedded.
context: Learning words in stories is 3x more effective than learning from lists. The narrative gives each word a memory anchor.
constraint_note: Read the story first, then test yourself on the vocabulary.
example: After reading, you should know: angadi, kaapi, bisi, sakkare, haal.

segments.text: Ramu woke up early and walked to the angadi — the small shop near his mane, his house.
He said: Namaskara! Ondu bisi kaapi kodi — one hot coffee please.
The shopkeeper asked: Sakkare beku? Do you want sugar?
Ramu said: Haudu, swalpa sakkare, mattu swalpa haal — yes, a little sugar, and a little milk.
He sat on a kurchi — a chair — and watched the beligge — the morning — wake up around him.
The kaapi was tumba chennagide — very good. Ramu smiled and said: Dhanyavada!

questions.prompt: What did Ramu ask for in his coffee?

action: Follow Priya's auto ride and learn the phrases she uses.
description: A story about Priya taking an auto-rickshaw, with common travel phrases embedded.
context: Auto ride phrases in a story context are easier to remember than a phrase list.
constraint_note: Pay attention to what Priya says at each step of the ride.
example: After reading, you should be able to handle a basic auto conversation.

segments.text: Priya stood at the road and waved at an auto. The driver stopped.
She said: Namaskara, Majestic-ge hogbeku — I need to go to Majestic.
The driver said: Eshtu doora — how far is it? She replied: Hattira, 3 kilometer — it's nearby, 3 kilometers.
He said: Nooru rupaayi — 100 rupees. She said: Tumba jaasti! Aivattu kodi — too much! Give 50.
They agreed on eppattu — 70 rupees. She got in and said: Nere hogi — go straight.
Near Majestic she said: Illi nilsi — stop here. She paid and said: Dhanyavada — thank you.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I need to go to' in Kannada?
How much did they agree on?

action: Read about Meera's visit to the market and learn food and shopping vocabulary.
description: A story about shopping at a local market with food vocabulary and numbers embedded.
context: Market vocabulary in a story sticks better because you can picture the scene.
constraint_note: Notice the food words and numbers used in the story.
example: After reading, you should know: hannu, tarkari, eerulli, tomato, aalu, and numbers in context.

segments.text: Meera went to the pete — the market — on Saturday beligge — morning.
First she bought tarkari — vegetables. Eradu kg aalu — 2 kilos of potatoes — and ondu kg eerulli — 1 kilo of onions.
The tarkari-wallah said: Tomato beku? Tumba fresh! She said: Haudu, ardhaa kg kodi — yes, give half a kilo.
Then she went to the hannu angadi — the fruit shop. She bought mooru baalehannu — 3 bananas — and eradu maavinhannu — 2 mangoes.
Bele eshtu? — How much total? The shopkeeper said: Nooru ippattu rupaayi — 120 rupees.
Meera said: Sari — okay, and paid. She carried her cheela — bag — home, happy with her fresh food.

questions.prompt: How many kilos of potatoes did Meera buy?
How much was the total bill?

action: Follow Kiran's day and notice how Kannada verbs change with tense.
description: A story about Kiran's daily routine that demonstrates present, past, and future tense naturally.
context: Grammar in stories is absorbed naturally — you hear the patterns without memorizing rules.
constraint_note: Notice the verb endings: -uttene (present), -de (past), -uttene with naale (future).
example: After reading, you should recognize present, past, and future tense patterns.

segments.text: Kiran's day started early. Ninne — yesterday — he got up at aaru gante — 6 o'clock. Avanu beligge eddha — he woke up in the morning.
Ivattu — today — he is busy. Avanu kelsa maaduttiddane — he is working. Avanu office-alli iddane — he is at the office.
At madhyahna — afternoon — avanu oota maadda — he ate lunch. Anna mattu saaru — rice and rasam.
Saanje — in the evening — avanu manege baruttane — he comes home. Avanu TV noduttane — he watches TV.
Naale — tomorrow — avanu Mysore-ge hoguttane — he will go to Mysore. Avanu snehitanige siguttane — he will meet his friend.
Kiran is happy. Ivattu kelsa, naale prayana — today work, tomorrow travel!

questions.prompt: What verb ending indicates past tense? (he woke up = eddha)
How do you know 'hoguttane' is future?

action: Read about Ananya's experience at a Karnataka home and learn about hospitality culture.
description: A story about visiting a Kannada family, demonstrating hospitality phrases and customs.
context: Cultural context makes phrases meaningful — you learn not just the words but when and why to use them.
constraint_note: Notice the hospitality sequence: invite, seat, offer drink, offer food.
example: After reading, you should understand Karnataka hospitality norms and key phrases.

segments.text: Ananya visited her colleague Lakshmi's mane — house — for the first time.
Lakshmi opened the baagilu — door — and said: Banni, banni! Olage banni! — Come, come! Come inside!
She said: Kootko — please sit. Then immediately: Kaapi beku? Chai beku? — Want coffee? Want tea?
Ananya said: Kaapi, dayavittu — coffee, please. Lakshmi brought bisi kaapi — hot coffee — and khaara mixture — spicy snacks.
Then Lakshmi asked: Oota aaytu? — Have you eaten? This is not really a question — it's an invitation to eat.
Ananya learned: in Karnataka, you never let a guest leave without offering food. Athithi devo bhava — the guest is God.

questions.answer: It's an invitation to eat (not just asking if you've eaten)
Invite in → seat → offer drink → offer food

action: Follow Suresh at the doctor's office and learn medical phrases.
description: A story about a doctor visit with health-related phrases embedded.
context: Medical phrases are critical safety vocabulary. A story makes them memorable.
constraint_note: Pay attention to how Suresh describes his symptoms and the doctor's responses.

segments.text: Suresh was not feeling well. Nanage hushaar illa — I'm not well, he told his wife.
He went to the aaspathre — hospital. He told the doctor: Doctor, nanage tala novu — I have a headache.
The doctor asked: Yaavaga shuru aaytu? — When did it start? Suresh said: Ninne raatri-inda — since last night.
The doctor asked: Jvara ide? — Do you have fever? Suresh said: Haudu, swalpa jvara ide — yes, a little fever.
The doctor said: Chinta maadabeda — don't worry. Aushadhi bareytene — I'll write medicine. Mooru dina rest maadi — rest for 3 days.
Suresh said: Dhanyavada, doctor. He took the aushadhi and went home to rest.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I'm not well' in Kannada?

action: Read about Divya's rainy day and learn weather and emotion words.
description: A story about a rainy day in Bangalore with weather and emotion vocabulary.
context: Weather and emotions are everyday conversation topics. A story connects them naturally.
constraint_note: Notice the weather words and how Divya's feelings change through the story.

segments.text: It was a mazhe dina — a rainy day — in Bengalooru. Divya looked out the kitiki — window.
The aakasha — sky — was kappu — black with mooda — clouds. Gaali was blowing — the wind was strong.
Divya felt swalpa bejaar — a little sad. She wanted bisilu — sunshine.
Then she made herself bisi kaapi — hot coffee. She sat near the kitiki and listened to the mazhe — rain.
Slowly she felt shanta — peaceful. The rain was sundara — beautiful. She felt khushi — happy.
Sometimes mazhe brings the best moments, she thought.

questions.prompt: What two emotions did Divya feel?

action: Follow Arjun as he gives directions to a lost tourist.
description: A story about giving directions, with direction and place vocabulary embedded.
context: Direction words in a real scenario are much easier to remember than in a list.
constraint_note: Trace the route in your mind as you read.

segments.text: A tourist stopped Arjun on MG Road. Kshamisi — excuse me, she said. Lalbagh yelli? — Where is Lalbagh?
Arjun said: Illi-inda nere hogi — from here go straight. Eradu signal nantara — after 2 signals.
Nantara bala tiru — then turn right. Ondu doddu raste baratte — a big road will come.
Aa raste-alli edakke tiru — on that road turn left. Aidu nimisha nadeyiri — walk for 5 minutes.
Lalbagh gate nimage kanisatte — you will see the Lalbagh gate. Idu hattira — it's nearby, tumba doora alla — not very far.
The tourist said: Tumba dhanyavada! — Thank you very much! Arjun smiled: Banni, welcome!

action: Read about Kavya's weekend plans and notice how she talks about past, present, and future.
description: A story demonstrating all three tenses naturally through weekend planning.
context: Hearing all three tenses in one natural conversation helps you internalize the patterns.
constraint_note: Notice: past (-de/-da), present (-uttene/-uttane), future (naale + verb).

segments.text: Kavya called her friend Nisha. Nisha, ninne naanu tumba kelsa maadde — yesterday I worked a lot, she said.
Ivattu naanu maneyalli iddene — today I am at home. Naanu rest maaduttiddene — I am resting.
Nisha asked: Naale enu plan? — What's the plan for tomorrow?
Kavya said: Naale naanu Cubbon Park-ge hoguttene — tomorrow I will go to Cubbon Park.
Neenu baruttiya? — Will you come? Nisha said: Haudu! Naanu baruttene! — Yes! I will come!
Kavya said: Chennagide! Beligge hattu gantege sigona — Great! Let's meet at 10 in the morning.

questions.prompt: Which word signals past tense in 'kelsa maadde'?
Which word signals present continuous in 'rest maaduttiddene'?

action: Read about Raj's first Ugadi celebration and learn about Karnataka festivals.
description: A story about experiencing Ugadi for the first time, with cultural vocabulary.
context: Festival stories teach culture and vocabulary together — the most natural way to learn.
constraint_note: Notice the festival-specific words and customs.

segments.text: Raj moved to Bengalooru from Delhi. His colleague invited him for Ugadi — the Kannada New Year.
At the mane — house — the baagilu — door — had a toran — a mango leaf decoration. Raj said: Tumba sundara! — Very beautiful!
His colleague's amma — mother — gave him bevu-bella. Bevu is neem — bitter. Bella is jaggery — sweet.
She said: Jeevana-dalli bevu-bella ide — life has bitter and sweet. We accept both. This is the meaning of Ugadi.
They ate holige — a sweet flatbread — and obbattu. Everyone said: Ugadi shubhashayagalu! — Happy Ugadi!
Raj felt like he belonged. He said: Nanna hosa mane, nanna hosa habba — my new home, my new festival.
